---
title: ""
shorttitle: ""
author:
  - name: Jordan B Gunn
    affiliations:
      - name: University of Cambridge
        department: Department of Psychology
keywords:
  - episodic memory
  - intrusive memories
  - selective interference
  - trauma-film paradigm
  - retrieved-context theory
  - computational modeling
author-note:
  disclosures:
    related-report: "This is an in-progress working draft. Please do not cite, quote, or circulate without the authors' written permission."
floatsintext: true
bibliography: references.bib
---

# Introduction

Traumatic experience highlights a trade-off between durability and selectivity in episodic remembering.
Memory should retain highly consequential episodes so critical details stay accessible long into the future.
At the same time, adaptive retrieval is selective: what comes to mind should normally depend on current cues, goals, and safety.
Intrusive memories after trauma suggest that durable encoding can outpace regulatory control.
The event returns repeatedly and indiscriminately, despite the demands of the present [@ehlers2000; @brewin2010; @krans2009; @iyadurai2019].

The present work examines this trade-off in the trauma-film paradigm [@james2016; @holmes2008].
Participants watch a distressing film depicting scenes of injury or threat [@holmes2008; @james2016].
Intrusive memories commonly emerge over the following days in diary measures [@holmes2004; @holmes2008; @james2016].
When asked to recall or recognize the same material deliberately, voluntary memory can be relatively preserved, and its relationship to intrusion frequency can be weak, such that the two measures dissociate [@holmes2004; @james2016].
The same encoded episode can therefore support both uncontrolled and controlled access, and these modes can come apart empirically [@holmes2004; @iyadurai2019].

A sharper dissociation emerges when a brief visuospatial task follows the film or a later film reminder [@holmes2009; @james2015; @kessler2020].
Across studies, participants who play Tetris or perform a similarly visually-engaging task report fewer intrusive memories over the following days [@holmes2009; @holmes2010; @deeprose2012].
Voluntary memory for the same material generally shows little measureable impact even when retrieval conditions are matched [@lauzhu2019; @asselbergs2023].
Such interventions are effective even after prompting reinstatement of real-life traumatic memories, reducing intrusions while preserving deliberate recall for life experiences such as motor vehicle accidents, traumatic childbirth following emergency caesarean section, and war-related trauma in refugees [@iyadurai2018; @horsch2017; @kanstrup2021].
A post-encoding or post-reminder manipulation appears to alter one mode of retrieval more than the other, even when both draw on the same recent experience.

A prominent interpretation attributes this selectivity to separable memory representations. [@brewin1996; @brewin2014].
Under this dual representation account, traumatic experience gives rise to distinct sensory and contextual traces, and a post-encoding task preferentially disrupts the sensory trace while leaving the contextual trace (and the voluntary memory it supports) relatively intact.
Here we develop an alternative within a unitary retrieved-context framework and demonstrate it in a formal model.
In this account, the degree of contextual overlap between the interference task and the original event governs retrieval competition, while the amount of control available at test governs which memories are expressed.
The dissociation between involuntary and voluntary recall emerges from these interacting constraints without requiring separate traces as a modeling prerequisite.
